explanation: This means that whenever prop_1 is true, prop_0 must also be true at that moment. This is a safety property asserting that in all moments where prop_1 holds, prop_0 also holds.
This formula expresses that whenever prop_1 holds, prop_0 must also hold at the same time. This matches the requirement that prop_0 shall occur if prop_1 occurs.
If prop_1 holds, then prop_0 must happen at some point in the future. In LTL, this is encoded as: whenever prop_1 is true, then eventually prop_0 will be true.

This specification means that whenever prop_1 is true, prop_0 must occur (at some point, possibly immediately or later). This is typically interpreted in LTL as: globally, if prop_1 holds, then eventually prop_0 will hold.
Whenever prop_1 is true, it is required that prop_0 also becomes true (either immediately or eventually after that point). So, globally, if prop_1 holds, then eventually prop_0 must hold.

Whenever prop_1 occurs, prop_0 should also occur (possibly at the same time or after). This can be captured using the 'if prop_1 then eventually prop_0' globally: whenever prop_1 is true, prop_0 must eventually follow.
Whenever prop_1 is true, prop_0 must also be true. This is a safety property, so it is expressed globally: at all times, if prop_1 holds, prop_0 must hold as well.

This requirement states that whenever prop_1 holds, prop_0 must eventually become true. In LTL, this is expressed as: whenever prop_1 is true, then in the future (possibly immediately), prop_0 will occur, which is written as G(prop_1 -> F prop_0).
Whenever prop_1 is true, then prop_0 must also be true at the same time. This is captured by the globally operator: G (prop_1 -> prop_0).
This means that whenever prop_1 is true, prop_0 must also occur (possibly at the same time or later). In LTL, this is often captured with "if prop_1 then eventually prop_0".
